text: 145 BPM speed garage in an Interplanetary Criminal / Y U QT lane.
Kick choice: use a fat 909-style / tech-house-style kick and tune it to the bassline or key.
In F minor, the bassline is built from only three functions — root, fourth, and sixth — repeated across different octaves.
The groove in the bassline comes from the rhythm and the octave jumps, especially the 16th-note lift into the higher note.

Reverse / sucking bass in Operator: three sine waves at different octaves, one slightly detuned, with the reverse effect created by an attack envelope on one oscillator and a little FM for extra bite.
Bass processing: low-pass filter plus Auto Pan used as sidechain simulation.
A quiet percussion loop can add a lot of energy in the mids and highs even when it is barely audible on its own.
Clap lands at the end of each bar, and the main hi-hat is made extremely short so it behaves almost like a 16th-note tick.
High-passed, sidechained house / percussion loops are crucial to the classic speed-garage feel.
Build the vocal atmosphere from one sample split into two complementary layers: one reverbed and sidechained, the other delayed and panned so the phrase opens out.
Use a third sparse punctuation vocal with delay, Auto Pan, and high-pass for occasional accents instead of keeping the vocal texture constant.
